summary: DeVault argues that free and open source software licenses explicitly disclaim all warranties and obligations, and that both users and maintainers need to internalize this fact. Users are not entitled to bug fixes, support, documentation, or any maintenance from FOSS maintainers. Equally important, maintainers must understand they have no obligation to burn themselves out serving an ever-growing user base. The post reframes FOSS as a social contract where users receive the freedom to modify and distribute software, not a guarantee of ongoing service. DeVault urges maintainers to say 'no,' manage expectations, and remember why they started their projects in the first place.
key_points: Every major open source license explicitly disclaims all warranties and responsibility for the software's fitness or quality
Users of FOSS are entitled to modify, distribute, and sell the software — but not to the maintainer's time, labor, or support
Maintainer burnout is driven by a misunderstanding of the social contract: communities pressure maintainers into responsibilities they never agreed to
Maintainers should be prepared to say 'no' and walk away when the work stops being fulfilling
Healthy FOSS projects require actively managing the social graph and expectations between maintainers and users
money_quotes: You are not entitled to the blood, sweat, and tears of the maintainers of the free & open source software you use.
FOSS is what you make of it.
You aren't entitled to the maintainer's time, but you are entitled to change the software, distribute your changes to others, and to sell the software with or without those changes.
As a maintainer, you need to be prepared to say "no". Working on your project should never feel like a curse.
You started it for a reason — remember that reason. Was it to lose your sanity? Or was it to have fun?
You can always walk away. You don't owe anything to anyone.
FOSS projects are made out of people, and maintaining that social graph is as important as maintaining the code.
key_insight: The warranty disclaimer in every open source license is not just legal boilerplate — it defines the social contract of FOSS, freeing both users and maintainers from obligations beyond the code itself.
